w:r.w:t: scale demonstrator of a Curved Canted Cosine Theta (CCCT) Dipole magnet

This paper reports on the manufacturing and assembly of a sub-scale prototype and a full-scale demonstrator of a curved Canted Cosine Theta (CCCT) dipole magnet at CERN’s main workshop. It outlines the challenges associated with the fabrication of curved formers and
joining technique and toolpath programming approach

. The successful manufacturing and assembly of both demonstrators confirm the feasibility of curved CCT magnets and highlight their potential for future accelerator applications and compact magnetic systems.
CCCT Magnets consist of two nested solenoids with opposed tilt angles
Simplified illustration of the CCCT concept, showing inner and outer superconducting windings and the particle beam passing through the aperture.
It has also attracted attention as a promising design for compact accelerators and gantry systems used in hadron therapy.
geometry offers considerable flexibility in generating complex field harmonics in both straight and curved configurations, where compactness is particularly
The Fusillo Project has been proposed for the HIE-ISOLDE Recoil Separator Ring
. The geometry of the full-scale demonstrator forms a 90° bend with a curvature radius of 1 m and an aperture of 236 mm.

. The scope of this paper is

the flanges contain the formers and provide

are wound on the inner former
Sufficient space for the insertion after winding is provided.
The conical shape is mirrored on inner and outer surfaces of the formers, resulting in a tapered coil. Exiting the inner former with a layer jump

the outer former. This assembly is then enclosed in a clamshell and connected with flanges at the extremities to hold against generated magnetic forces, bending the assembly. The fabrication procedure of the formers, the most complex component, is explained in the following section.
Representation of the inner former (dark blue), outer former (light blue), and coil (dark grey), with the layer jump (brown) depicted in an exploded view.
Further assembly steps by the TE-MSC-SMT section
included the precise positioning of cables
of the design before proceeding with the full
EN-MME engaged in the production of the inner and outer formers for the
The machining sequences consisted of four steps:
Horizontal setup on V-clamps for roughing and finishing interior and end surfaces
ertical setup with additional blocks fixed in the chuck, secured internally using a threaded rod
for roughing and finishing the exterior envelope
Horizontal setup on additional blocks to allow machining of the outer surface, including cable channels and features such as the connection box

w:r.w:delText: allowing the machining of superconducting cables channels.

w:r.w:t: A key manufacturing challenge in roughing the formers from pre-cored billets, as encountered for
ISOLDE cavities [5], was the use of very long tool extensions (over 600 mm for interior machining), which were prone to chatter.
First Setup for interior roughing and milling with Long toolholder attached.
sure positioning accuracy over the entire process, two
have been manufactured and adapted to fit into the apertures
well-defined reference geometries to execute control and measurement cycles with the machines integrated 3D measuring probe.
They also serve as fixture tooling

w:r.w:t: eference block can be seen on the left part of the item.
The outer formers of the sub-scale Fusillo were manufactured on a 5-axis machine with a rotating table. This capability was extensively used during
finishing: after a highly dynamic roughing cycle removed most of the material,
leaving a 2 mm allowance, a 20 mm carbide ball-nose mill performed
in a single continuous spiral, with simultaneous 5-axis motio
Vertical positioning of the component with positioning blocks on top and bottom
The full-scale demonstrator was designed using a segmented approach, with spanning angles of 35°, 30°, and 25° for both the outer and inner formers.

w:r.w:delText: is provided in the table below (

w:r.w:t: Representation of the full-scale inner former made of 3 segments.
machining the internal surfaces of each segment
. Once completed, all segments were assembled, after which the external surfaces were machined to achieve the final
adapted because finishing the 90° former in a vertical setup proved impractical due to
machine angle limitations. Consequently, the machining of the external surfaces was carried out using a horizontal setup.
or the assembly, positioning was ensured by machining two cylindrical contact surfaces
on the sides of each segment,
to achieve concentric alignment, along with a dowel pin to control azimuthal positioning.
Initial trials employed thermal fitting using liquid nitrogen, based on the principle of shrink-fitting; however, this approach did not provide sufficient resistance to separation
The adopted solution involved injecting Stycast epoxy resin into orbital grooves located at the interfaces between segments. Once cured, the epoxy acts as five
circumferential (orbital) pins, thereby providing the required mechanical retention and positional stability
Illustration of the assembly technique, showing the machined grooves and holes for Epoxy Resin injection, cross section view.
LOCTITE STYCAST EE 4215/HD 3561 was selected as the injection resin due to its favo
83 provides the necessary stability for precise alignment within the machined channels, acting
In conclusion this process offered favourable parameters
conservative friction coefficient (µ = 0.25), along with no adhesive bonding between the resin and aluminium.

w:r.w:delText: nder assumption of a conservative frictional coefficient (µ=0.25) and no adhesive bonding between r
separation of the two former sections, o

w:r.w:t: FEM simulation depicting the distribution of tensile load applied to the assembly, plotting stress under applied forces.
The joint was validated on a full
were machined using a standard slot-milling tool with a rounded geometry (r = 1 mm) and a depth of 0.3 mm, resulting in a cross-sectional area larger than in the simulation
plastic deformation and an elastic separation of less than 5 µm
This mechanical stability is crucial not only
for subsequent handling of the assembly but also for withstanding the bending forces induced by the magnetic field.
Destructive testing of the full-scale cylindrical mock-up to complete separation.
The resin was injected into opposing holes from the interior of the assembly using a 3D-printed applicator (
), while two additional holes allowed air to escape from the interior.
Assembly of the full-scale former with the 3D printed applicator visible inside the part.
Machining of the outer surface and channels

After assembly into the full 90° former, machining of the outer surfaces and channels was carried out on a Waldrich-Coburg Taurus 30 in three successive stages. The process began with the top side, where roughing was performed using a high-torque HSK-A100 machining head, followed by semi-finishing with an HSK-A63 high-speed head.

Equivalent operations were then carried out on the bottom side, where final surface finishing, as well as the milling of grooves and features, was completed. In the final stage, the remaining grooves and features on the top side were machined, thereby completing the component.
Machining of the Channels and finishing of the

During the roughing step, probing measurements were performed on reference surfaces machined on the exterior of the formers in order to align the component with the metrology-derived reference frame, which had previously been established through a best-fit alignment of the interior surfaces.
Additionally, auxiliary metal plates featuring machined pockets were attached to the faces and served as reference surfaces throughout the machining sequence
For the subsequent machining steps, a custom fixture was manufactured in situ, without dismantling the part, to ensure stable clamping and precise positioning within the machine.
at three defined key points corresponding to the support locations
to minimize vibration as effectively as possible during machining.
This fixture was re-machined between the semi-finishing and finishing stages
to accommodate the change in dimensions
For the finishing of the channels, a custom-made end mill was

w:r.w:delText: procured from a local supplier specializing in bespoke tooling solutions.

w:r.w:t: This tool was specifically designed to ensure smooth, rounded transitions at both the bottom and shoulder of the channels, featuring a radius of r = 0.25 mm at both the tip and shank, thus adhering to the specified tolerances of the geometry in one
Former after all machining operations were performed.

Due to the conical geometry of the formers, the channel layout does not exhibit repetitive patterns. Given the large number of geometrical features to be manufactured, a programmatic approach was adopted for toolpath definition (
also provided an extended level of control over the toolpaths, exceeding the capabilities of conventional CAM software.

enabled the generation of all required toolpaths from a single procedure, including 5-axis pilot drilling, roughing and finishing of the grooves, as well as transitional toolpaths to ensure smooth connections between grooves machined from opposite sides. To prevent overtravel of the machine’s rotary C-axis, the toolpaths were segmented at predefined angular intervals.
The toolpath definition was derived from data extracted directly from the CAD model, including the centreline of
and the corresponding surface normal vectors, using the open-source software FreeCAD, which supports advanced macro programming. These data were then used to generate toolpaths through
computations, requiring only minimal positional adjustments of the tool within the groove geometry

. The resulting toolpaths were subsequently imported into the commercial CAM software WorkNC by encoding them in the appropriate format, enabling them to be recognized as native toolpaths. This allowed the custom-generated toolpaths to benefit from WorkNC’s built-in capabilities, such as collision detection and post-processing, while maintaining full control over the toolpath geometry. This hybrid approach effectively combined the flexibility of custom-defined toolpaths with the robustness and usability of advanced CAM software.
Visual representation of the toolpath at a given point, illustrating the normal, tangential, and binormal directions defining the local toolpath coordinate frame

w:r.w:delText: using the Creaform MetraSCAN 3D system. This high-precision optical CMM scanner
selected for its ability to deliver reliable measurements on

Geometric deviations were observed between the inner and outer surfaces, primarily near one side of the former, likely due to repositioning during assembly. These deviations were considered acceptable, as they do not impact functionality or integration, and therefore no additional correction steps were deemed necessary.
The finished outer former in metrology.

w:r.w:t: The manufacturing and assembly of the Fusillo curved canted cosine theta (CCCT) dipole magnet represent a significant achievement in precision engineering under complex geometric and mechanical constraints.
it was possible to produce both sub
scale prototypes that meet the requirements for superconducting magnet formers.
but also lays the groundwork for
The Fusillo project thus stands as a template of the collaborative and interdisciplinary approach
transform a challenging theoretical design into a manufacturable and functional physical prototype.

w:r.w:delText: We would like to express our sincere gratitude to the TE-MSC-SMT section for their theoretical insights and foundational work, which significantly influenced the manufacturing strategies employed in this project.

We also thank the IFT TU Wien, headed by Prof. Friedrich Bleicher, for their valuable academic review. Furthermore, we appreciate the staff at LBNL Berkeley, California, for the fruitful exchange of knowledge
. Finally, we acknowledge the contributions of the external subcontractors responsible for the manufacturing of the clamshell and flanges, as well as the preparation of stock material.
The assembled Fusillo Magnet inside the clamshell, ready for initial testing at CERN’s SM18 test facility. (May 2025)

w:r.w:t: ., “Hi-Lumi LHC twin-aperture orbit correctors magnet system optimisation,”
., “Superconducting curved canted-cosine-theta (CCT) for the HIE-ISOLDE recoil separator ring at CERN,”
., “Curved canted-cosine-theta (CCCT) dipole prototype development at CERN,”
., “Transient behavior of the second Fusillo subscale curved CCT magnet,”

w:r.w:delText: ., “Design and test of a curved superconducting dipole magnet for proton therapy,”
Nuclear Instruments and Methods in Physics Research
., “Progress in the fabrication of the RFQ accelerator for the CERN Linac4.” Available: https://cds.cern.ch/record/1303326/files/CERN-ATS-2010-224.pdf
